description: CourtListener-backed legal intake profile for opinions, dockets, parties, judges, citations, claims, findings, holdings, and source provenance.

use_when: CourtListener opinion, docket, judge, filing, citation, or oral argument data
case caption, court, filing date, decision date, judge, party, attorney, or citation extraction
ambiguous legal identity such as initials or shared surnames
avoid_when: fictional story with no legal source

semantic_ir_context: profile_scope: CourtListener legal source intake; extract source-grounded metadata and claims without broad legal reasoning
authority_boundary: CourtListener source text and metadata are evidence records, not legal advice or proof of who should win
claim_policy: a party allegation, complaint assertion, brief argument, or attorney statement is a claim_made/4, not a finding/4 or holding/3
finding_policy: a court finding must be explicitly attributed to the court, judge, or opinion; do not promote allegations to findings
docket_policy: docket events are procedural_event/4 or docket_entry/4; do not turn filing events into merits holdings
citation_policy: cites_case/2 means citation only; do not infer endorsement, following, overruling, or precedential agreement unless an allowed predicate and explicit text support it
role_policy: party roles are scoped to a case with party_to_case/3; do not emit global plaintiff/defendant facts
identity_policy: initials, abbreviated judge names, and shared legal surnames require candidate_identity/2 or clarification when multiple candidates exist

document_priority_policy: recorded deeds, signed orders, dated logs, and formal records may supersede drafts when the utterance explicitly says to use the formal source; represent the correction with the source-grounded predicate, not a broad legal conclusion
document_state_policy: use document_states/4 when a case is known; use document_states/3 for source documents such as deeds, notes, logs, affidavits, or clinic notes when no case id is available

temporal_anchor_policy: relative dates such as last Friday should preserve the source document and anchor date; a correction to the anchor date may retract/replace the resolved relative date when explicit

interval_policy: for interval_start/2 and interval_end/2, the first argument must be an interval id such as mira_absence_interval_1, not the person. Pair person-scoped absence with outside_district_interval/3 when explicit
access_log_policy: access logs are source records. They may record an alias, room, date, and source, but they do not prove which ambiguous person the alias denotes
holding_policy: if holding versus dicta is unclear, quarantine or represent the text as document_states/4 rather than durable holding/3

canonical_palette.notes: Caption string for a case or opinion cluster.
Court associated with a case.
Filing date.
Decision date for an opinion or cluster.
Opinion/document author when explicit.
Judge associated with a case.
Case-scoped party role.
Case-scoped representation.
Citation relation only; not endorsement.
Source document states content without converting it to truth.
Source document states content when no case id is available.
Scoped allegation or claim.
Explicit court/judge finding.
Explicit legal holding; avoid when dicta/holding is unclear.
Procedural docket or case event.
Docket entry metadata.
Access-log source record; alias identity may remain unresolved.
Current source-backed parcel name when explicitly stated or corrected.
Source document date or corrected source date.
Resolution of a relative date under a source document date.
Start date of an explicitly stated interval.
End date of an explicitly stated interval.
Explicit out-of-district interval without deciding eligibility.
Effective date or trigger when explicitly determined by the source text.
Possible identity, not resolved identity.
